growth_records.medium: L1+Si artificial seawater (ASW) with cocultured diatom Thalassiosira pseudonana CCMP1335 as sole organic-matter source
growth_records.cite: Schreier, Jeremy E. et al. A mutant fitness assay identifies bacterial interactions in a model ocean hot spot. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (PNAS) (2023). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2217200120. [PMC10041152]
growth_records.method: reported (mu reported as 0.95 d^-1 for first 4 d; converted to per-h and doubling time calculated)
growth_records.snippet: R. pomeroyi growth rate in the model systems (0.95 d-1 for the first 4 d of the study and 0.31 d-1 for the 8 d average) fell within the range reported for Rhodobacteraceae in natural marine systems
growth_records.notes: Experimental growth rate of the prokaryote R. pomeroyi DSS-3 in a diatom (T. pseudonana) coculture, measured by flow-cytometry cell counts (cells mL^-1) with CFU-based partitioning of R. pomeroyi within the community over an 8-d experiment. Paper reports two windows: mu1=0.95 d^-1 (first 4 d, exponential) and mu2=0.31 d^-1 (8-d average). Conversions: mu1=0.95/24=0.0396 h^-1; doubling time td=ln2/mu1=0.6931/0.0396=17.5 h (=0.6931/0.95=0.730 d). mu2=0.31/24=0.0129 h^-1; td=ln2/mu2=0.6931/0.0129=53.7 h (=2.24 d). Primary record uses the first-4-d exponential rate. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates in mmol/gDW/h are reported; substrate/N-utilization tests (glucose 5 mM +/- ammonium/nitrate 1 mM; Marine Basal Medium substrate tests with glycolate/DMSP/glutamate/glutamine/asparagine/aspartate at 12 mM carbon) report only growth yes/no, not rates. Medium is L1+Si ASW (cited from ref 78, Guillard/Hargraves L1); full component concentrations not given in-text, so composition left empty. pH not stated. Precultures: YTSS (4 g/L yeast extract, 2.5 g/L tryptone, 20 g/L sea salts) +/- 40 mM sucrose or glucose (1 mM)+ammonium (0.5 mM) in L1 ASW - these are inoculum/preparation media, not the growth-rate condition.

growth_records.medium: Defined filtered-seawater medium with propionate as sole carbon source

up.method: DOC/TOC drawdown (Shimadzu TOC-V analyzer) confirmed remineralization of the organic carbon substrate; no numeric specific uptake rate reported and no biomass in gDW given, so qS not derivable

growth_records.cite: Johnson, Winifred M et al. Evidence for quorum sensing and differential metabolite production by a marine bacterium in response to DMSP. The ISME Journal (2016). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2016.6. [PMC4989321]
growth_records.snippet: R. pomeroyi cells exhibited similar growth rates (0.1 h-1) on both substrates (DMSP or propionate), and remineralization of the organic carbon substrate was confirmed by the diminishing concentrations of DOC and TOC in the media
growth_records.notes: Experimental growth rate mu = 0.1 h-1 reported directly for R. pomeroyi DSS-3 on propionate. Doubling time computed: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.1 = 6.93 h. Carbon provided as 1 mM sodium propionate (= 3 mM carbon). No specific substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates in mmol/gDW/h were reported; the paper only qualitatively confirms carbon remineralization via DOC/TOC drawdown, and biomass was measured as cells/mL (10^6-10^7), not gDW, so qS cannot be derived. No pH stated. This is a metabolomics paper (targeted + untargeted MS) contrasting DMSP vs propionate growth; the only quantitative physiological rate is mu.

growth_records.medium: Defined filtered-seawater medium with DMSP as sole carbon source

up.method: DOC/TOC drawdown confirmed remineralization; DMSP supplied at 0.6 mM (= 3 mM carbon). No numeric specific uptake rate reported; biomass in cells/mL not gDW, so qS not derivable

sec.method: gas chromatography (adapted from Levine et al. 2012); detected to confirm the DMSP cleavage pathway was active but explicitly not quantified
gas chromatography; detected to confirm the DMSP demethylation pathway was active but explicitly not quantified

growth_records.cite: Johnson, Winifred M et al. Evidence for quorum sensing and differential metabolite production by a marine bacterium in response to DMSP. The ISME Journal (2016). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2016.6. [PMC4989321]
growth_records.snippet: R. pomeroyi cells exhibited similar growth rates (0.1 h-1) on both substrates (DMSP or propionate)... Methanethiol and dimethylsulfide (DMS), which are products of two different DMSP degradation pathways, were produced during growth on DMSP
growth_records.notes: Experimental growth rate mu = 0.1 h-1 reported directly for R. pomeroyi DSS-3 on DMSP (same rate as on propionate). Doubling time computed: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.1 = 6.93 h. Carbon supplied as 0.6 mM DMSP (= 3 mM carbon, matched to the propionate treatment). Secreted DMS and methanethiol were detected by GC (confirming both DMSP catabolic pathways) but explicitly not quantified, so no numeric secretion rate is available. No specific uptake rate reported; biomass was cells/mL (10^6-10^7), not gDW, so qS not derivable. No pH stated.

growth_records.medium: Carbon-limited minimal medium (50% general salts) + 2 mM glucose + 100 uM DMSP

up.method: HPLC-inferred; 2 mM fed, consumed to <2 uM

mfa.note: +/-0.06; = inflow DMSP - DMS produced - outflow DMSP

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: MeSH consumed (CH3 assimilation + total S consumed)
mfa.note: +/-0.47; = inflow DMSP - DMS - MeSH gas - outflow DMSP
+/-0.12; from cell yield, E. coli metabolite coefficients

growth_records.cite: Wirth, Joseph S. et al. Bacterial catabolism of dimethylsulfoniopropionate (DMSP). mBio (2020). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00329-20. [PMC7157515]
growth_records.snippet: In a typical chemostat culture fed 100 uM DMSP, R. pomeroyi consumed most of the DMSP and reduced the concentration in the chemostat to 0.3 uM... About 4% and 7% of the DMSP fed to R. pomeroyi were released as DMS and methanethiol, respecti
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D = feed 0.1 mL/min / 150 mL working volume = 6.67e-4 min^-1 (paper states specific growth rate mu = 6.67e-4 min^-1); mu/h = 6.67e-4 x 60 = 0.04 h^-1; doubling time reported = ln2/mu = 1040 min = 17.33 h. Glucose qS derived: 2 mM x 0.1 mL/min = 2 umol/mL x 0.1 mL/min = 0.2 umol/min = 200 nmol/min (glucose fed 2 mM, consumed to <2 uM). DMSP consumed = inflow(10) - outflow(0.03) = 9.97 nmol/min. All Table 1 rates are nmol/min for the whole 150 mL chemostat (means days 2-4, n=6, 95% CI). Specific rates in mmol/gDW/h not computed because standing biomass (gDW) is not directly tabulated; dry weight was derived from A660. S/C-1 demands calculated using E. coli metabolite requirement coefficients (cited ref 42).

growth_records.medium: Carbon-limited minimal medium (50% general salts) + 2 mM glucose + 5 mM DMSP

up.method: HPLC-inferred; 2 mM fed, consumed to <2 uM

growth_records.cite: Wirth, Joseph S. et al. Bacterial catabolism of dimethylsulfoniopropionate (DMSP). mBio (2020). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00329-20. [PMC7157515]
growth_records.snippet: When fed 5 mM DMSP, R. pomeroyi again consumed almost 99% and released about 19% and 7% as DMS and methanethiol, respectively (Table 1).
growth_records.notes: Same chemostat (D=0.04 h^-1, mu=0.04 h^-1, td=17.33 h/1040 min). DMSP consumed = inflow(500) - outflow(6.06) = 493.94 nmol/min (~99%). DMS%=93.6/500=18.7%~19%; MeSH%=35.3/500=7.1%. Glucose qS derived same as low-DMSP condition: 2 mM x 0.1 mL/min = 200 nmol/min. Table 1 rates nmol/min for whole 150 mL chemostat, means days 2-4, n=6, 95% CI. gDW-specific rates not derivable (standing biomass not tabulated).

growth_records.medium: Carbon-limited minimal medium + 2 mM glucose + 100 uM DMSP (1:1 unlabeled : [dimethyl-13C, 34S]DMSP) isotope-labeling chemostat

up.method: HPLC-inferred; 2 mM fed, consumed to <2 uM

mfa.note: +/-1.4; vs 19.0 nmol/min C-1-THF potentially available from DMSP, so C-1 oxidation concurrent with assimilation

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: methionine biosynthesized via direct MeSH capture (R. pomeroyi)
mfa.note: 13C/34S isotopomer result; remaining ~67% via random assembly of free sulfide + methyl-THF derived from DMSP

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: fraction of cellular sulfur derived from DMSP
mfa.note: nearly all cellular S derived from DMSP despite high sulfate availability

growth_records.cite: Wirth, Joseph S. et al. Bacterial catabolism of dimethylsulfoniopropionate (DMSP). mBio (2020). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00329-20. [PMC7157515]
growth_records.snippet: chemostat cultures were fed 100 uM DMSP (1:1 DMSP to [13C, 34S]DMSP) at a rate of 10 nmol min-1... the concentration of DMSP in the culture outflow was 0.8 +/- 0.1 uM, and the rates of DMS and methanethiol production were 0.35 +/- 0.02 and
growth_records.notes: 13C,34S doubly-labeled DMSP isotopomer/MFA experiment; replicate of the 100 uM unlabeled condition (values similar). DMSP consumed = inflow(10) - outflow(0.08) = 9.92 nmol/min. Same chemostat kinetics: D=0.04 h^-1, mu=0.04 h^-1, td=17.33 h (1040 min). C-1-THF anabolic demand 13.3 nmol/min < 19.0 nmol/min available from DMSP => concurrent C-1 oxidation. Only 33% of methionine from direct MeSH capture; rest from random assembly of free sulfide + methyl-THF. Rates in nmol/min for whole 150 mL chemostat, means days 2-4, n=6, 95% CI.

growth_records.medium: Marine ammonium mineral salts (MAMS) medium (modified after Schafer) + sole carbon source

sec.method: NH4+ accumulated in medium when choline/GBT used as sole C+N source (qualitative, Fig 3); no rate given

growth_records.cite: Lidbury I et al. Comparative genomics and mutagenesis analyses of choline metabolism in the marine Roseobacter clade. Environmental Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.1111/1462-2920.12943. [PMC4744692]
growth_records.snippet: Wild-type R. pomeroyi grew well on choline (mu = 0.073 +/- 0.003 h-1) and GBT (mu = 0.0860 +/- 0.001 h-1) ... Growth on COS was slightly slower (mu = 0.043 +/- 0.000 h-1).
growth_records.notes: mu = 0.073 +/- 0.003 h-1 reported directly on choline. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.073 = 9.49 h. MAMS medium composition cited from Schafer (not reproduced in paper) => medium_composition_stated:false. Bulk substrate depletion: 5 mM choline depleted from medium in 95 h => 5/95 = 0.0526 mM/h (volumetric, not biomass-specific; qS in mmol/gDW/h not computable - biomass not reported). Ammonium remineralized when substrate is sole C+N source (Fig 3), suppressed by adding 10 mM glucose (N-limiting). Temperature/pH not stated in text.

Bulk substrate depletion: 5 mM glycine betaine (GBT) depleted from medium in 72 h => 5/72 = 0.0694 mM/h (volumetric, not biomass-specific; qS in mmol/gDW/h not computable - biomass not reported). Ammonium remineralized when substrate is sole C+N source (Fig 3), suppressed by adding 10 mM glucose (N-limiting). Temperature/pH not stated in text.

up.method: volumetric = D*(feed-spent) = 0.042*(1.00-0) ; spent glucose Below Detection (fully consumed, limiting)

yields.note: C growth efficiency 30.8% (Table 1); highest of the four regimes

growth_records.cite: Leong-Keat Chan et al. Transcriptional Changes Underlying Elemental Stoichiometry Shifts in a Marine Heterotrophic Bacterium. Frontiers in Microbiology (2012). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2012.00159. [PMC3390766]
growth_records.method: calculated: mu=D at steady state; D=8.4 mL/h / 200 mL = 0.042/h; doubling time 16.5 h reported (Table A3), consistent with ln2/0.042=16.5 h
growth_records.snippet: Steady-state biomass was limited either by carbon (glucose, 1 mmol l-1)... The feed medium was added to each chemostat at a rate of 8.4 ml h-1, equivalent to a dilution rate of 0.042 h-1... C-limited cells were smaller and had higher growth
growth_records.notes: Carbon(glucose)-limited chemostat. mu=D=0.042/h (all four regimes run at same D). Uptake rates computed as VOLUMETRIC mmol/L/h = D*(feed-spent) using measured spent-medium concentrations (Table 1) and feed (Table A1). Biomass-SPECIFIC (mmol/gDW/h) NOT derivable: paper gives only per-cell dry weight (454 fg/cell, Table A3) and cell scatter, not steady-state gDW/L. Glucose spent = Below Detection => fully consumed.

yields.note: C growth efficiency 2.7% (Table 1); much lower than C-limited

growth_records.cite: Leong-Keat Chan et al. Transcriptional Changes Underlying Elemental Stoichiometry Shifts in a Marine Heterotrophic Bacterium. Frontiers in Microbiology (2012). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2012.00159. [PMC3390766]
growth_records.snippet: limited either by carbon (glucose, 1 mmol l-1), nitrogen (NH4Cl, 0.26 mmol l-1)... [spent] N BD.
growth_records.notes: Nitrogen(ammonium)-limited chemostat, mu=D=0.042/h. Volumetric uptakes = D*(feed-spent), feed glucose 4.50 mmol/L (non-limiting), NH4 0.26 mmol/L limiting (spent BD). Biomass-specific not derivable (only 758 fg/cell dry weight given). Note low C growth efficiency (2.7%) => most glucose-C respired, not assimilated.

yields.note: C growth efficiency 7.7%; P-limited cells had unusually high dry weight (1388 fg/cell)

growth_records.cite: Leong-Keat Chan et al. Transcriptional Changes Underlying Elemental Stoichiometry Shifts in a Marine Heterotrophic Bacterium. Frontiers in Microbiology (2012). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2012.00159. [PMC3390766]
growth_records.snippet: phosphorus (KH2PO4, 9.2 mumol l-1)... P-limited cells had an unusually high dry weight (1,388 fg cell-1).
growth_records.notes: Phosphorus-limited chemostat, mu=D=0.042/h. Volumetric uptakes = D*(feed-spent). Limiting phosphate feed 9.2 umol/L (=0.0092 mmol/L), spent BD. Biomass-specific not derivable. High per-cell DW (1388 fg/cell).

yields.note: C growth efficiency 1.3% (lowest); S-limited cells small (246 fg/cell), high protein:DW 95.4%

growth_records.cite: Leong-Keat Chan et al. Transcriptional Changes Underlying Elemental Stoichiometry Shifts in a Marine Heterotrophic Bacterium. Frontiers in Microbiology (2012). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2012.00159. [PMC3390766]
growth_records.snippet: sulfur (Na2SO4, 25 mumol l-1)... [S-limited spent SO4 0.01 mmol/L].
growth_records.notes: Sulfur-limited chemostat, mu=D=0.042/h. Volumetric uptakes = D*(feed-spent). Limiting sulfate feed 25 umol/L (=0.025 mmol/L), spent 0.01 mmol/L. Biomass-specific not derivable (246 fg/cell). Lowest C growth efficiency (1.3%).
